text: Let's pause on 1. What Is Open Source? What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 1. What Is Open Source? is still useful because open source software is software whose source code is publicly available. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: Anyone can read it, use it, and - in most cases - contribute to it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Fixing bugs in the software. Writing or improving documentation. Filing bug reports that help maintainers understand problems. Reviewing other people's changes and leaving thoughtful feedback.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put 2. Who Can Contribute? into plain language. Contributors come from all backgrounds, skill levels, and countries. The useful version is: A first contribution could be fixing a typo, adding a missing full stop, or filing a bug report that saves a maintainer hours of debugging.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 3. What Makes a Good First Contribution? What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: 3. What Makes a Good First Contribution? has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Specific - it addresses one problem clearly, not a general "this could be better". Scoped - it does not try to fix everything at once; one PR, one problem. Described - the PR or issue explains what changed and why, not just what. Tested - for documentation, this means reading it aloud with your screen reader before submitting; for code, it means verifying the fix works.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because a well-executed small contribution is far more valuable than a large contribution that cannot be merged because it is out of scope. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. The PR touches more than three or four files. You need to understand the entire codebase to make the change. The issue has been open for a long time with many comments suggesting it is complex.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 4. Finding Something to Work On. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: 4. Finding Something to Work On: Most open source projects label issues that are suitable for new contributors. The next useful detail is concrete: How to search: On any GitHub repository, go to Issues → filter by label.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Learning Cards: Finding Something to Work On has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. On a repository's Issues page, use the landmark shortcut (D in NVDA browse mode) to jump to the filter region, then type a label name like good first issue in the Label filter field and press Enter to narrow results. GitHub's global search (label:"good first issue" is:open language:markdown) returns a results list navigable by heading level -- each issue title is a link, so press K (next link) or Tab to step through them efficiently. Before claiming an issue, press End to jump to the bottom of the issue page and listen for recent comments -- if someone already said "I'll take this," move on to the next one. GitHub color-codes labels, but do not rely on color alone -- hover over a label to see its text name in a tooltip, or use the Label dropdown which lists label names as text. Zoom to 150-200% when scanning the Issues list; the issue title, label pills, and comment count remain in a single row up to about 250% zoom before wrapping. Use the Sort dropdown ("Newest," "Recently updated") to push stale issues down the list so you focus on actively maintained work first.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does someone need before they touch the keyboard?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 5. Reading an Issue Before You Start: before commenting "I'll take this" on an issue, ask yourself. That matters in practice: If the issue looks right for you, comment briefly to let the team know you are working on it: "I'd like to work on this.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Is the description clear enough to act on? If you are not sure what the problem is, ask a clarifying question before starting work. Is anyone else already working on it? Look for recent comments from others saying they are working on it, or an open PR that references this issue. If a PR exists, it may already be in review. Is the issue in scope for me? A documentation task does not require programming knowledge. A bug fix in compiled code may require understanding the codebase. How old is the issue? Very old issues (2+ years) may be stale or no longer relevant. You can ask the maintainer if it is still valid before investing time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Tool Cards: Fork, Clone, and Contribute. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Tool Cards: Fork, Clone, and Contribute has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Click Fork on the repository page, then Create fork; Edit files directly in your fork's web interface; Click Contribute Open pull request to submit back to the original; and Fork the repo on github.com first. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave Tool Cards: Fork, Clone, and Contribute, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is navigate to your fork and press. to open in the web editor. Step two is edit, commit, and create a PR from the Source Control panel. Step three is fork on github.com, then clone your fork: Ctrl+Shift+P Git: Clone. Step four is create a branch, make edits, commit and push. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like gh repo fork owner/repo --clone; cd repo; git checkout -b fix/my-change; edit files; git add. && git commit -m "fix: description"; git push -u origin fix/my-change; gh pr create. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on The Basic Workflow. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The Basic Workflow has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is fork the repository - creates your own copy on GitHub. Step two is clone your fork to your computer (or open a Codespace - see Appendix N). Step three is create a branch - name it something descriptive: fix/broken-link-setup-guide. Step four is make your change - edit the file, save, verify. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave The Basic Workflow, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, commit with a clear message - "Fix broken link in setup-guide.md line 34". Then, push to your fork. After that, open a pull request from your branch to the original repository's default branch. Finally, respond to review feedback - maintainers may ask for changes; this is normal and not a rejection. Keep it that plain: know where you are, make the move, check the result.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Do not treat Writing a Good PR Description as decoration. Example: Fixed a broken link on line 34 of setup-guide.md. That is not trivia. The link pointed to /docs/old-setup which no longer exists.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. What did you change? Why was the change needed? How did you verify it works?

speaker: JAMIE
text: What do you want them to do when the plan breaks?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 7. Getting Help is still useful because it is always acceptable to ask a question on an issue or pull request. For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: If you opened a PR and are waiting for a review, it is appropriate to leave one polite follow-up comment after a week or two.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Are specific: "I'm trying to fix the broken link on line 24 of setup-guide.md. The link currently points to /docs/old-setup. Where should it point?". Show what you tried: "I searched the repository for the correct URL but couldn't find a file at that path.". Are polite: Assume good intent from maintainers, even if they are slow to respond. Maintainers are often volunteers with day jobs.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Put 8. After Your Contribution Is Merged into plain language. This matters for your GitHub profile. The useful version is: Each merged contribution demonstrates real-world collaboration with a project team: you scoped a problem, communicated with maintainers, addressed feedback, and saw the work through. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Your name appears in the project's commit history permanently - it cannot be removed. The issue you fixed is closed. You are officially listed as a contributor to this project, visible on the repository's Contributors page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 9. Building a Contribution Habit. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. The hardest part of open source contribution is starting. That is the difference between guessing and knowing: Once you have one merged PR, the next is easier - you know the workflow, you have proof it is possible, and you have already navigated the social dynamics of working with a maintainer.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because challenge Time: Complete Challenge 8: The Culture Layer in the Challenge Hub, then advance to Chapter 09: Labels, Milestones and Projects. That gives the learner a foothold: next: Chapter 09: Labels, Milestones, and Projects Back: Chapter 07: Merge Conflicts Related appendices: Appendix M: Accessibility Standards Appendix F: Git Security Appendix O: Branch Protection.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Keep a list of projects you use and like. These are natural candidates for contributions because you already understand what they do. File bug reports when you encounter problems, even if you cannot fix them yourself. A clear, reproducible bug report is a real contribution. Review other PRs. Even as a new contributor, you can leave useful feedback: "Does this change affect screen reader users?" or "The example in the PR description is missing a step.". Set a low bar. A contribution does not need to be impressive. A fixed typo merged into a project used by thousands of people is more valuable than a perfect contribution never submitted.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the final checkpoint?

speaker: ALEX
text: You should be able to point to the evidence, explain the action, and describe what you would do next if this were a real open source project. If you can teach the move back, you have learned it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if they get stuck?

speaker: ALEX
text: Read the latest message, not the loudest worry. Check the issue, the branch, the pull request, the status check, or the bot comment. Then ask for help with those facts in hand. That is how professionals collaborate.
